Late Antiquity

Augustine of Hippo

354 – 430 CE

The turn inward: memory, will, and the self as an interior world answerable to truth.

At the center of Augustine’s anthropology is a double conviction: the human soul is the created principle of life, and the mind is the soul’s highest, rational, self-reflexive power. Yet the mind is never, for Augustine, the highest reality. It knows truth only because immutable truth stands above it; it becomes morally divided when the will turns from higher to lower goods; it experiences time as a stretching and scattering of attention; and it bears the image of God most fully when memory, understanding, and love are turned toward God. Read together, Confessions X–XI, De libero arbitrio, and De Trinitate yield one integrated account of inwardness, freedom, temporality, and restoration by grace.

A preliminary terminological caution is necessary. Augustine does not always use anima, mens, animus, and spiritus with perfectly rigid technicality across his corpus, but a stable pattern does emerge. Anima names the soul broadly as the inward life-principle of the human being; mens names the superior, rational part of that soul; and “spirit” can be used in several senses, so Augustine sometimes has to clarify that by “the spirit of the mind” he means the mind itself. In Confessions X he can say that in man there appear both body and soul, the one outward and the other inward; in City of God IX he explicitly calls the mind the superior part of the soul; and in On the Soul and its Origin he insists that the soul is not bodily, not made from God’s own substance, and not something whose precise origin in each individual case he can confidently explain.

1. The basic structure of Augustine’s view

The soul, for Augustine, is a creature and therefore good, but mutable. This is one of his most consistent claims, from early anti-Manichaean works to later mature theology. In On the Nature of Good he argues that every nature, as nature, is good, and that evil is not a substance but a corruption of measure, form, and order. In Of Two Souls he insists that every soul, insofar as it is a living thing, has its source in God, while in City of God XIII he adds the important qualification that the soul is immortal in the sense that it does not simply lapse into non-being, even though it can suffer a kind of “death” by alienation from God. Thus Augustine’s doctrine of the soul is anti-dualist in a precise sense: the soul is neither a fragment of divine substance nor one half of a cosmic struggle between two coeternal principles.

From this follows a second thesis: the mind is higher than sensation, imagination, and bodily life, but it is not self-grounding. In Confessions VII Augustine describes his inward turn as an ascent into himself, where he sees above his own mind the unchangeable light. In De libero arbitrio II he argues that we judge even our own minds by reference to an unchangeable truth that we do not judge, which means that truth is superior to our reasoning power. In De Trinitate XII he says that higher reason judges corporeal things according to incorporeal and eternal reasons above the human mind. Augustine’s interiority is therefore never a closed immanence: to go inward rightly is already to be led beyond oneself.

A third foundational point is that Augustine’s account of the soul is inseparable from love and will. The soul is not merely a knower; it is an ordo amoris, a being whose life is determined by what it loves and how it loves. In City of God XIV he argues that the various passions are, at bottom, modes or inflections of will under right or wrong conditions. In De libero arbitrio I the good will is treated as a higher good than worldly power or bodily pleasure, and true freedom is described not as arbitrary self-assertion but as secure enjoyment of the stable good, that is, of truth and God. Augustine’s psychology is thus decisively moral: cognition, affection, and willing interpenetrate.

2. The prologue in Confessions VII–VIII

Although your focus is rightly on Confessions X–XI, Book VII is indispensable because it gives the metaphysical and epistemological prologue to those later books. Augustine recounts receiving “books of the Platonists,” which taught him to seek incorporeal truth and enabled him to make the inward ascent. He then says that, returning into himself, he saw above his own mind the unchangeable light. This is the classic Augustinian scene of illumination: the soul discovers both its dignity and its limit at once. It is capable of ascending beyond sensible things, but what it sees is not its own essence; it sees a truth and light superior to itself.

At the same time, Augustine marks the insufficiency of Platonism with real precision. Those books helped him see that God is incorporeal, that the Word is divine, and that created reality depends on immutable truth. But they did not teach him that the Word became flesh, nor the humility and healing grace by which the proud soul is restored. So even at this early stage Augustine’s account of the mind is already anti-self-redemptive. Philosophical ascent is real, but it is not enough; the mind needs mediation, healing, and conversion, not just metaphysical clarification.

Book VIII then provides the moral-psychological counterpart to Book VII’s noetic ascent. There Augustine does not describe ignorance so much as divided willing. His famous analysis runs from perverse will to lust, from lust indulged to habit, and from habit unresisted to necessity. He says there are “two wills,” old and new, carnal and spiritual, contending within him, and he asks how the mind can command itself and yet be resisted by itself. The crucial point is that the split is not between two substances, but within one soul whose willing is partial and infirm.

This is one of Augustine’s great anti-Manichaean moves. The conflict within the self is real, but it is not evidence for two souls or two natures. Augustine says explicitly that those who infer two opposed minds from opposed wills are mistaken; the phenomenon is rather one soul fluctuating between conflicting loves. This analysis becomes the bridge to De libero arbitrio: evil comes from the will’s aversion, not from an evil substance lodged inside us.

3. Confessions X: memory, self-knowledge, and God

Book X is not merely another stage in autobiographical narrative. It is Augustine’s sustained examination of what the mind is now, in the present, before God. He begins by asking to know God as he is known, and he insists that confession happens coram Deo: before the God to whom conscience is already open. Human self-knowledge is therefore not autonomous introspection but a theologically situated inquiry. He can speak of the “inner man,” but that inner man is readable only in the light of the One who knows him more deeply than he knows himself.

The governing theme of the book is memory. Augustine famously describes memory as vast and almost immeasurable, and he even says, in one of the strongest formulations in the work, that this thing is the mind and this is what he himself is. Memory contains sensory images, conceptual knowledge, arts, numerical and rational truths, and the traces of inward life. It is not just an archive of past sense-data. It is the interior field in which the soul can retrieve, compare, judge, and think. The sheer scale of the description matters: memory is Augustine’s first great phenomenological map of inwardness.

What makes the analysis especially profound is that memory stores not only “objects” but operations and affections. Augustine says memory retains the affections of the mind in its own way, so that he can remember joy without currently rejoicing or recall sorrow without currently suffering it. He also remarks that he remembers having understood, remembers having remembered, and remembers distinguishing truth from falsehood. This reflexive structure is essential: the mind is not merely turned toward external things but is recursively present to its own acts. Augustine is already moving toward the triadic analyses of De Trinitate.

The analysis of the “happy life” deepens the point. Augustine argues that all human beings desire happiness, and that this universal desire implies some kind of remembered or interiorly known access to what happiness is. Yet happiness is not bodily pleasure, eloquence, or any finite attainment. He concludes that the happy life is joy in God and, more sharply still, joy in the truth. This means the deepest dynamism of the mind is not neutral curiosity but a teleological hunger for truth and beatitude. The mind is structured by desire for the true good.

The most subtle part of Book X is Augustine’s treatment of God and memory. He says that from the time he learned God, God abides in his memory and is found there when he calls him to mind. Yet he also says he does not find God among bodily images, nor among the mind’s affections, nor as though God were simply one thing contained in a mental chamber. God is not a bodily image, not an affection, not even the mind itself; Augustine finally says he found God “above” him, and then erupts into the confession that God was within while he himself was outside. The implication is decisive: memory is the site of recollection, but God is not contained within it as one more item. God is inwardly present while also transcending the mind that remembers him.

Book X is equally important because it refuses any naïve doctrine of self-transparency. Augustine repeatedly admits that he does not know himself securely. He says his mind hesitates to trust itself when investigating its own powers, and at one point confesses that he has become a puzzle to himself. The long examination of food, sexuality, music, curiosity, praise, and dreams shows why: the self is opaque because it is morally unstable, embedded in habit, and vulnerable to temptation. The book ends not in triumph but in prayer for continence, with the striking thought that continence gathers us back into one from the dispersion in which we had been scattered into many.

That final note is crucial for understanding Augustine’s view of mind. The mind is vast, reflexive, and elevated above bodily life; but it is also wounded, divided, and dispersed. Inwardness is not yet unity. The very powers that make the mind noble also make it painfully aware of its fragmentation. This is why Book X already leans toward the temporal analysis of Book XI and the trinitarian analysis of De Trinitate: the mind must be understood as both structured and disordered, powerful and needy, interior and yet not at rest in itself.

4. Confessions XI: time and the distention of the soul

Book XI is often read as a treatise on time in abstraction, but Augustine’s argument is inseparable from anthropology. He begins by insisting on divine eternity as distinct from all temporal succession: in God nothing passes away, the whole is present, and time itself is created. Hence the question “What was God doing before creation?” is misconceived, because there was no “before” before time. His famous question, “What then is time?”, is thus framed by a contrast between the Creator’s still eternity and the creature’s passage.

Augustine’s decisive move is to relocate temporality into the soul. Past and future, strictly speaking, are not present as such; what exists is a threefold present: the present of past things, which is memory; the present of present things, which is attention or sight; and the present of future things, which is expectation. Time is therefore not first encountered as an external entity that the mind later observes. It is encountered through the way the mind holds together remembrance, present attention, and anticipation. This is one of the deepest ways in which Augustine links mind and time.

He then asks how we measure time if the past is no longer and the future is not yet. His answer is that what we measure is not an independently subsisting past or future, but the trace or impression left in the mind as things pass. The analysis of syllables and verses is meant to show exactly this: I can judge a long syllable against a short one only because what has sounded leaves something retained in the mind while present attention engages what is now occurring. Time is measured in the mind’s retention and passage, not in the bodies as such.

The psalm example gives the existential core of the argument. Before recitation, the whole psalm lies in expectation; as it is recited, part slips into memory, part remains in expectation, and the present act of attention transfers what is future into what is past. Augustine then scales this up from a psalm to a human action, from an action to a whole life, and from a life to the age of humankind. The famous distentio animi—the stretching or distention of the soul—is therefore not just a linguistic puzzle. It names the condition of finite consciousness as dispersed across what is no longer, what is not yet, and what barely is. This is why Augustine can say that his life is a distraction, and that only through the Mediator can he be recollected from the many into the One.

This is one of Augustine’s most important contributions to the doctrine of mind. The soul is not merely a substance with faculties; it is a temporally stretched life. Memory and expectation are not accidental additions to an otherwise static self; they are constitutive of how the mind inhabits created existence. Yet Augustine does not absolutize this condition. Temporal distention is also, in spiritual terms, a mark of fallenness and dispersion. The soul is stretched because it is not yet fully gathered. So the analysis of time is at once phenomenological and soteriological.

5. De libero arbitrio: will, evil, and immutable truth

De libero arbitrio begins from the problem of evil, but its answer becomes a full theory of soul, will, and rational ascent. Augustine first distinguishes two senses of evil: evil done and evil suffered. God, as just, can be the author of punitive evils suffered by sinners, but not of moral evil itself. Moral evil must have an author, and that author is not a substance or cosmic principle but the evil will of rational creatures. Augustine says plainly that evildoers are the authors of their evildoing, and that punishment would not be just unless the evil had arisen through the will.

In Book I he also gives one of his strongest valuations of the will. The good will is said to be better than kingdoms and bodily pleasures, and the happy person is the one who delights in a good will and regards unstable goods as of lesser worth. Augustine defines good will as the will by which we seek to live rightly and honorably. This is immensely important for his doctrine of the mind: moral excellence is not primarily a matter of possessing information, nor of externally conforming to law, but of the will’s right orientation toward the good. Virtue is the flourishing of a rightly ordered will.

Book II broadens the inquiry from ethics to epistemology. Augustine constructs a hierarchy running from bodily objects to the bodily senses, from the senses to the inner sense, and from the inner sense to reason, which judges all the lower levels. But reason itself is not ultimate. Augustine argues that there is an unchangeable truth common to all minds, not private to “mine” or “yours,” and that we judge our own minds in accordance with this truth without being able to judge the truth itself. Here again the structure is the same as in Confessions VII: the mind rises above bodily life precisely by discovering a light above itself.

The moral metaphysics that follows is characteristically Augustinian. The mind becomes a slave to lust only through its own will; evil is the soul’s voluntary turning away from the Creator toward lower, created goods; vice is not a natural substance but a corruption within a good created nature; and real freedom is submission to truth, which is to say to God. Augustine therefore rejects two opposite errors at once. On the one hand, evil is not a nature. On the other hand, freedom is not independence from the highest good, but secure delight in it. To enjoy losable goods as ultimate is bondage, not liberty.

The later Augustine does not abandon this account, but he deepens it by insisting more sharply on grace. Already in the later retrospective discussion attached to the Cambridge volume, Augustine says the Pelagians misread the earlier work: unless the will is set free by God’s grace from slavery to sin, it cannot live rightly. Likewise, in City of God V he argues that God’s foreknowledge does not abolish free will, because our wills themselves belong within the order of causes foreknown by God. So the mature position is not a repudiation of free choice, but a refusal to treat created willing as self-healing. Free will is real, good, and responsible; grace is what restores it.

6. De Trinitate: the mind as image of God

If Confessions X–XI gives Augustine’s introspective phenomenology, De Trinitate gives his most architectonic doctrine of mind. The governing question is how the human being, and especially the mind, is made in the image of the triune God. In Book IX Augustine begins with the triad of mind, its knowledge of itself, and its love of itself. Because the mind is incorporeal, it knows itself through itself; and when the triad is properly grasped, these three are not accidental companions but one life and one essence under relational distinctions. The psychological analogy begins from self-presence and self-love.

Book X develops the analysis into the more famous triad of memory, understanding, and will. Augustine says these are not three lives or three minds, but one life, one mind, one essence. He also insists on their mutual inclusion: memory remembers itself and the others, understanding understands itself and the others, will embraces its own acts together with the others. Most strikingly, Augustine argues that the mind always in some way remembers, understands, and loves itself, even when it is not explicitly thinking about itself. Human inwardness is thus deeper than occurrent consciousness; the mind is tacitly self-present before it becomes reflectively self-aware.

Book XII introduces one of Augustine’s most useful distinctions for anthropology: higher and lower reason, or wisdom and knowledge. The mind is one, not divided into two substances, but it has one operation directed toward eternal things and another directed toward the management of temporal, bodily, and practical affairs. Augustine says the higher reason judges corporeal matters by eternal reasons above the human mind, while the lower operation concerns temporal use and action. The image of God is not denied to the whole mind, but it is found especially where the mind cleaves to contemplation of the eternal rather than merely managing temporal affairs.

Book XIV is decisive for Augustine’s mature doctrine. There he says the image of God remains in the mind even when worn and defaced by sin, because the mind is image precisely in being capable of God and able to partake of God. But he then adds a crucial correction: the mind is not image merely because it remembers, understands, and loves itself. It is most properly image because it can remember, understand, and love the God by whom it was made. In doing so it becomes wise; in failing to do so it remains foolish even if it is reflexively self-aware. The image is therefore structural, but also teleological and theological. It exists as capacity and vocation, not as a closed formal pattern.

The same book also gives Augustine’s doctrine of renewal. The image is not replaced by another image; it is the same image that is healed and renewed. Forgiveness removes guilt, but the wound of the soul is healed gradually as the inner man is renewed in the knowledge of God. Augustine compares this to the distinction between removing a weapon and healing the wound it caused. The mind therefore has a history: created as image, deformed by sin, renewed by grace, and perfected in the vision of God. The culmination of anthropology is beatific participation, not mere self-possession.

Book XV finally adds an indispensable caution. Augustine explicitly notes that memory, understanding, and love in us are “mine”: it is I who remember by memory, understand by understanding, and love by love. That is, the psychological triads do not yield three human persons or independent subsistences. The analogy to the Trinity is real, but it remains only an image and a trace, not an identity of structure. This warning matters because it prevents De Trinitate from collapsing the divine mystery into a mere faculty psychology. The human mind mirrors the Trinity analogically, and inadequately, precisely as creature.

7. The broader Augustinian corpus

The line of thought already appears in the early Soliloquies. There Augustine frames his inquiry around two objects above all: God and the soul. He prays, “let me know myself; let me know you,” and invokes God as truth, wisdom, life, blessedness, and intelligible light. Even here the basic structure is present: self-knowledge and knowledge of God belong together, and God is the illuminating source by which the soul comes to know what it is. The mature doctrines of illumination, inwardness, and beatitude do not emerge abruptly in the later works; they are present from the start in seed form.

The anti-Manichaean writings sharpen Augustine’s metaphysical commitments. In Of Two Souls he rejects the claim that there are two opposed souls in one person and insists that every soul as life has its source from God. In On the Nature of Good he argues that every nature is good insofar as it exists, that evil is corruption rather than substance, and that sin is not pursuit of an evil nature but desertion of a better. He can even say there that the rational soul ought not to be “in its own power” but in subjection to God. These works are not peripheral to Augustine’s doctrine of mind; they provide the anti-dualist grammar without which his later psychology cannot be understood.

On the Soul and its Origin and the later books of City of God add further nuance. Augustine there insists that the soul is mutable and therefore not from God’s own substance, that it is not bodily, and that he does not possess certain knowledge about whether individual souls are newly created or propagated. In City of God XIII he says the soul is truly immortal in that it does not cease living and feeling, yet it can suffer its own death by forsakenness from God. In City of God IX and XIV he also says that the mind is the superior part of the soul and that the passions are to be governed by it, while those same passions are themselves forms of willing under right or wrong conditions. So Augustine’s picture is not a simple dualism of intellect versus body. It is a hierarchy within a single embodied human life.

The anti-Pelagian writings then press the therapeutic and theological implications of everything that came before. In On Grace and Free Will Augustine continues to affirm free choice; indeed, he says Scriptural precepts would be unintelligible without it. But in On the Spirit and the Letter he insists that free will before liberation is a name without genuine liberty, and in On the Predestination of the Saints he famously remarks that he had laboured on behalf of free choice, but grace prevailed. This does not erase the earlier anthropology. It specifies its condition of possibility after the fall: the mind and will remain real powers, yet their healing is a gift.

8. Augustine’s appropriations and rejections of earlier philosophies

Augustine’s closest philosophical appropriation is from Platonism, especially in its late antique form. From the Platonists he takes the ascent from sensible to intelligible reality, the rejection of crude materialism, the emphasis on immutable truth, and the conviction that the soul must turn inward in order to rise above the bodily. But he Christianizes each of these themes. The inward turn leads to a light above the mind, not merely to the mind’s own highest power; participation replaces autonomous ascent; and the decisive truth absent from Platonist books is that the Word became flesh and heals pride by humility. He also rejects theories of recollection and transmigration in De Trinitate XII. Augustine is therefore deeply Platonizing without ever becoming simply a Platonist.

His most sustained rejection is of Manichaeism. Against it he denies that evil is a substance, denies that there are two contrary souls in one person, denies that the soul is a material fragment of divine light, and denies that bodily reality as such is evil. Created natures are good; evil is privation and disorder; moral evil arises from will; and the soul is God’s creature, not God’s substance. This anti-Manichaean framework is not merely polemical background. It is the condition for Augustine’s entire account of mind, freedom, and grace.

His relation to Stoicism is more selective. Augustine shares the Stoic concern that reason should govern passions, and in City of God IX he can describe the mind as the superior part of the soul that ought to bridle lower perturbations. Yet he refuses both Stoic self-sufficiency and Stoic fatalism. In City of God XIV he interprets passions as modes of will rather than mere irrational disturbances, and in Book V he insists that divine foreknowledge does not abolish voluntary causality. The mind is not autonomous rational control; it is a created power that must be subjected to God if it is to rule itself and the lower movements rightly.

Conclusion

The most economical way to state Augustine’s doctrine is this: the soul is the created, inward, living principle of the human being; the mind is the soul’s highest rational power; and that mind is defined not only by its capacities but by its relation to what is above it. It remembers, understands, and wills; it can turn inward; it can judge lower realities; but it does not ground itself. Truth judges it, time stretches it, love directs it, sin disorders it, and grace heals it. The human mind is an image of God because it is capable of God and made for God.

So Augustine’s view of mind is neither a simple metaphysics of an immortal soul nor a mere proto-psychology of inner experience. It is an integrated Christian anthropology in which ontology, epistemology, ethics, temporality, and soteriology are inseparable. The mind finds itself only by being led beyond itself; God is at once more inward than the mind’s own inwardness and higher than its highest reach; and the perfected human person is not the one who finally possesses himself in isolation, but the one whose memory, understanding, and love have been recollected into the truth and peace of God.

If it would help, I can turn this into a more formal research-paper style document with sectioned references to the exact books and chapters of Augustine’s works.
